title: The beginning
assignment: Look over the ideas from this section. If something isn't clear, go back to the lesson.
groups.0.title: The rule
groups.0.items.0.def: The selector says WHICH elements. The curly braces say how far it reaches. Inside sits a property, a colon, a value, a semicolon. Four parts — and each one breaks in its own way.
groups.0.items.1.def: A CSS error gets no message, no line number, no red. The browser discards what it can't parse and moves on. A missing semicolon kills two lines. A wrong kind of value kills only its own line. A missing brace kills nothing — it swallows the next rule inside itself and turns it into "this inside that", alive and matching nothing. The silence is deliberate: a page with one broken character should still render.
groups.0.items.2.def: Every property accepts a certain kind of value. `color` wants a colour, `font-size` wants a size. Give a size to a colour and the line is thrown away silently. And the other way round: `darkblue`, `#00008b` and `rgb(0, 0, 139)` are the same colour written three ways.
groups.1.title: Three places for the same style
groups.1.items.0.def: Applies to that one element. Three elements means writing it three times. Not forbidden — just almost never the right choice.
groups.1.items.1.def: One rule for the whole page. The second page of the site knows nothing about it — you'd copy it over. Useful while you're experimenting.
groups.1.items.2.def: The normal way. Thirty pages write the same one line and get the same styles. The browser keeps the file once it has downloaded it — that's why the second page opens faster. `rel` says what the relationship is, `href` is the path, the tag closes itself.
groups.2.title: You don't start from nothing
groups.2.items.0.term: The browser has its own CSS
groups.2.items.0.def: It's applied before yours arrives. It decides the heading is twice the size, the list has discs, the link is blue and underlined, the body has 8 pixels of margin. The numbers come from the W3C sample stylesheet of 1998 and were copied from there into every browser.
groups.2.items.1.def: So a page whose author wrote no styles is still readable. Insurance, not aesthetics. Which means your first job is sometimes to take it off. A *reset* strips everything and returns the elements to a bare start. A *normalize* keeps what's useful and only fixes the differences between browsers. Not synonyms.
groups.2.items.2.term: Take something away, owe a replacement
groups.2.items.2.def: An underline on a link isn't decoration. It's the only thing that tells a link from text for someone who can't tell the colours apart. Remove it and you owe them another signal.
